svg.title: The Guide to Wireframing — first 20 pages (FrameGraph replica)
svg.desc: Structural replica of pp. 1–20 of UXPin's “The Guide to Wireframing” (Chris Bank). Body text is the real extracted copy; the book's photographic/sketch figures are the authors' copyrighted illustrations, shown as labelled image-placeholders. The cover's wireframe mock is rebuilt from primitives. Layout is representative, not pixel-exact.

text.tspan: There’s a lot of chatter about high-fidelity, functional prototypes, and MVPs
these days. While that sounds sexy in theory, product teams can’t just go from
0 to 100mph in most cases - instead of making a Minimum Viable Product (MVP),
you’ll make a Sucky User Experience (SUX).
What I’m seeing, however, is a convergence of tools for wireframing and
prototyping, a blurring of traditionally distinct stages in the product
development cycle to create a broadened spectrum of wireframe styles that allow
teams to communicate at the level of detail and speed required.
Okay, so there’s probably only a few kinds of wireframes you’ll ever use. But
if you count variations of medium and level of detail, you could probably get
